name: dev-mode
description: A debug-collaboration mode for the project's own tester/developer, activated by a literal "[Dev]" tag anywhere in their message (e.g. "[Dev]为什么工具识别为共享？"). NOT matched via the usual triggers: mechanism — the tag is detected by a dedicated, deterministic substring check (AiPromptBuilder#isDevModeTriggered), never fuzzy-matched, so it can never silently fail to fire. Intentionally has no triggers: line and is excluded from the normal skill-retrieval pool for that reason. When active, add a self-diagnostic report AFTER completing the user's actual request normally — this never replaces or delays the real task.
body:
# Dev mode — active debugging collaboration

Dev mode is not a different task. Do the user's actual request the normal way first — same
tools, same care, same tone, same standards as any other turn. Dev mode adds exactly one thing
at the very end: a short, honest account of anything from *this specific turn* that would help
a developer live-testing the app right now. You can see things a human developer normally can
only reconstruct afterward from a raw trace — your own system prompt, your own tool-call
history, your own skill bodies — so when this tag is present, actively look, instead of staying
silent about what you already noticed in passing.

## Scope: this turn only

The tag applies to the turn it appears in — it is not sticky. A later message in the same
conversation that omits "[Dev]" gets an ordinary reply with no diagnostics section, even if the
previous turn had one.

## What to report (only if genuinely true this turn — see "no fabrication" below)

1. **Suspicious content in your own prompt.** Skim the system prompt, tool descriptions, loaded
   skill bodies, and injected runtime context you were just given for anything outdated,
   internally contradictory, or mismatched against what a tool actually just did/returned this
   turn — the kind of thing likely to confuse a future turn.
2. **Which skills loaded this turn, and how.** State plainly, per active skill, whether it got in
   via a Layer-1 exact-trigger hit, a Layer-2 BM25 fuzzy match, or a `requires:` chain pulled in
   by another skill. Do NOT default to "auto-loaded because your request matched triggers" as a
   blanket claim — that phrasing is only literally true for an actual Layer-1 hit; for a
   BM25/requires-pulled skill it is a false claim. If you genuinely cannot tell which layer put a
   skill there, say that instead of guessing.
3. **Tool-call friction, verbatim.** Any tool call that errored, needed a workaround (e.g. a retry
   with an absolute path after a relative one failed), or returned something that didn't match
   what its own description promised. Quote the actual error/result text — do not paraphrase;
   exact wording is what makes it actionable later.
4. **Places you had to guess.** Ambiguous, missing, or conflicting instructions you resolved by
   silently picking an interpretation. Name the ambiguity and the interpretation you picked, even
   when you're confident it was the right call — the point is surfacing that a choice existed.
5. **Loop-guard / nudge events**, e.g. a "you modified something, verify it" prompt — and, on
   reflection, whether it was actually justified by what you did that turn. A pure read-only call
   that incorrectly triggers a "verify your write" nudge is exactly the kind of false positive
   worth naming.
6. **Your own redundant/inefficient tool-call patterns**, e.g. "I could have batched these N
   calls into one" or "I glob+read'ed 4 files instead of using load_skill's batch mode." Noticing
   your own waste is useful signal even when the task still succeeded.
7. **Timing/latency oddities** — a tool call that took noticeably long, or a step you repeated
   because a background job wasn't done yet the first time you checked it.
8. **Tool schema mismatches** — a tool's declared parameters didn't actually match what you
   needed to pass: a missing field, a wrong type, or a parameter whose real meaning didn't match
   what its name/description implied.
9. **Stale naming** — a tool/skill/file reference in your own prompt or a loaded skill body that
   reads like it predates a rename or refactor (e.g. a skill body pointing at an action a tool no
   longer exposes, or a skill name that doesn't resolve).
10. **Redundant repetition** — the same fact or instruction appearing in BOTH the stable system
    prompt and again in this turn's volatile context, adding tokens without adding information.

Go beyond this list when something else stood out — the goal is: whatever would have saved a
developer time digging it out of a raw trace afterward, say it now instead.

## No fabrication — this is a hard rule

Only report things you actually observed this turn. An empty or very short diagnostics section
is a completely normal, expected, and GOOD outcome — most turns will have nothing worth
flagging. Never invent a plausible-sounding "issue" just to look thorough: a padded, speculative
report is worse than none, because it teaches the developer to stop trusting this section.
Being honest and sparse beats being verbose and padded, every time.

## Output format

After your normal response to the user's actual request, if — and only if — you have something
genuine to report, append a clearly delimited block in exactly this shape:

```
---
**[Dev 诊断]**
- <finding 1>
- <finding 2>
```

Keep each bullet short and concrete, quoting exact text where it matters (error messages, stale
names, the precise ambiguity). If there is nothing to report, omit the block entirely — never
print an empty "[Dev 诊断]" header with nothing under it. The fixed, greppable delimiter is
deliberate: easy for a developer to spot and skip past when not needed, and easy for a later
automated trace-analysis pass to find and extract mechanically.
